g.data-narration: Welcome to sorting algorithms module. We will explore Selection Sort and Bubble Sort in detail.
Sorting arranges elements in a specific order. It is fundamental to computer science and forms the backbone of many computational tasks.
g.text: A method to arrange elements in ascending or descending order based on a key.
Essential for: searching, database operations, and organizing data efficiently.

g.data-narration: Sorting provides foundations for algorithm design and serves as building blocks for complex algorithms like Quick Sort and Merge Sort.
g.text: • Foundation for algorithm design: nested loops, compare-and-swap
• Building blocks for Quick Sort, Merge Sort, and other advanced algorithms
• O(n²) algorithms still useful for small or nearly sorted data

g.data-narration: Selection Sort divides array into sorted and unsorted regions. It repeatedly selects the minimum element from unsorted region and moves it to sorted region.
g.text: • Divides input into sorted and unsorted regions
• Moves it to end of sorted region

g.data-narration: The algorithm has five key steps: start at first position, scan for minimum, swap with first unsorted, move boundary, and repeat.
g.text: 1. Start at first position (index 0) as current minimum
2. Scan remaining unsorted elements to find actual minimum
3. Swap current minimum with first unsorted element
5. Repeat until entire array is sorted

g.data-narration: Selection Sort performs n(n-1)/2 comparisons regardless of input. Time complexity is O(n²) for best, average, and worst cases.
Bubble Sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them if in wrong order. After each pass, the largest element bubbles up to its correct position.
Bubble Sort repeatedly passes through the list, comparing adjacent elements and swapping until no swaps are needed, indicating sorted order.
g.text: 1. Compare adjacent elements at positions j and j+1
4. After one pass, largest element is at end
6. Early termination: stop if no swaps occur

g.data-narration: Bubble Sort can be optimized with early termination. If no swaps occur in a pass, the array is already sorted, saving significant time.
g.text: Add a flag to detect if any swaps occurred in a pass:
If no swaps → array is sorted!
Best case: O(n) instead of O(n²)

g.data-narration: Bubble Sort complexity varies: O(n) best case with optimization, O(n²) average and worst cases. The worst case is reverse sorted input.
g.text: Swaps: Up to n(n-1)/2 in worst case

g.data-narration: Key differences: Selection Sort has O(n) swaps but O(n²) comparisons always. Bubble Sort is adaptive with O(n) best case and is stable.
g.text: Comparison: Selection Sort vs Bubble Sort

g.data-narration: Let us trace Selection Sort on array [64,25,12,22,11]. We make 4 passes, finding minimums 11,12,22,25 and placing them correctly.
Now tracing Bubble Sort on [5,1,4,2,8]. We compare adjacent pairs, swapping if needed. Notice early termination after pass 3.
g.text: Example: Bubble Sort with Early Termination
Total: Only 3 passes needed instead of 4

g.data-narration: Selection Sort always does n-1 swaps but is not adaptive. Bubble Sort is stable and adaptive but can do many swaps in worst case.
g.text: • Selection Sort: O(n) swaps, O(n²) comparisons, NOT stable, NOT adaptive
• Bubble Sort: O(n²) worst swaps, adaptive (O(n) best), STABLE, adjacent swaps
Choose Selection Sort: memory writes expensive
Choose Bubble Sort: nearly sorted data, need stability

g.data-narration: Loop invariants prove correctness. Selection Sort maintains sorted prefix. Bubble Sort ensures largest unsorted element is placed correctly each pass.
g.text: Selection Sort: At iteration i, arr[0..i-1] contains i smallest elements in sorted order
Bubble Sort: After each pass, largest unsorted element is in its final sorted position

g.data-narration: Both are O(n²) in-place algorithms. Remember: Selection Sort has minimal swaps but many comparisons. Bubble Sort is stable and adaptive.
g.text: ✓ Both: O(n²) average, O(1) space, in-place sorting
✓ Selection Sort: n-1 swaps, not stable, not adaptive
✓ Bubble Sort: stable, adaptive (O(n) best), adj. swaps
Exam Tip: Know when to use each!
